name: Circular trail running through the viewpoints of the Tejera Negra Black Beechwood Natural Park
name_original: Circular trailrunning por los miradores del Parque Natural del Hayedo Tejera Negra
region: National Park del Hayedo Tejera Negra
region_intro: Jogging routes around National Park del Hayedo Tejera Negra traverse a landscape characterized by ancient beech forests, river valleys, and diverse flora. The park, a UNESCO World Heritage site, features a unique microclimate sustained by the Lillas and Zarzas rivers, which flow through its valleys. Runners can experience varied terrain, from well-maintained paths alongside waterways to more challenging ascents leading to panoramic viewpoints. This natural space offers a setting for running amidst significant ecological features. Best jogging routes around National Park del Hayedo Tejera Negra The most popular jogging route is Circular trail running through the viewpoints of the Tejera Negra Black Beechwood Natural Park, a 6.5 miles (10.4 km) trail that takes 1 hour 30 minutes to complete. This difficult route offers views across the beech forest. Another top favourite among local runners is Parking in front of the Lillas River – Tejera Negra Beech Forest loop from Aparcamiento Hayedo Tejera Negra, a difficult 4.1 miles (6.6 km) path. This running route follows the Lillas River through the dense beech forest. Local runners also love the Cerro Gordo – Mirador de Peñas Llanas loop from Ermita de Hontanares, a 8.0 miles (12.8 km) trail leading through varied terrain with significant elevation gain, often completed in about 2 hours 15 minutes. Jogging around National Park del Hayedo Tejera Negra is defined by ancient beech forests, river systems, and panoramic viewpoints. The network offers options for various ability levels, including easy, moderate, and difficult running routes. The routes in National Park del Hayedo Tejera Negra are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 4 reviews. More than 400 runners have used komoot to explore National Park del Hayedo Tejera Negra's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes can I find in National Park del Hayedo Tejera Negra?
A: There are over 35 dedicated running routes available in National Park del Hayedo Tejera Negra. These routes cater to various skill levels, from easy paths to more challenging trails with significant elevation changes.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on the jogging routes in the park?
A: The terrain varies, offering a mix of well-maintained paths, sections alongside the Lillas and Zarzas rivers, and more challenging ascents. You'll primarily run through ancient beech forests, oak groves, and open areas, providing a diverse and engaging experience.

Q: Are there any easy running routes suitable for beginners or families?
A: Yes, while many routes are considered difficult, there are a few easier options. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1031392176/circular-trail-running-through-the-viewpoints-of-the-tejera-negra-black-beechwood-natural-park" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Circular trail running through the viewpoints of the Tejera Negra Black Beechwood Natural Park&lt;/a&gt;, for example, is a popular choice that offers a manageable distance and stunning views, though it is rated difficult due to elevation. For a generally accessible experience, the Senda de Carretas, a circular path starting from the park's parking lot, is often recommended for its comfortable paths and suitability for families.

Q: What are some notable natural features or viewpoints I can see while running?
A: The park is renowned for its ancient beech forest, a UNESCO World Heritage site, which provides a unique backdrop. You can also enjoy the Lillas and Zarzas rivers, which carve through the valleys. For panoramic views, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5438565" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Matarredonda Meadow Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; offers stunning vistas of the entire beech forest from 1600 meters above sea level.

Q: When is the best time of year to go jogging in National Park del Hayedo Tejera Negra?
A: While beautiful year-round, the park is particularly spectacular in autumn when the beech leaves transform into vibrant yellows, reds, and browns, creating a breathtaking backdrop for your run. The microclimate created by the rivers also ensures a fresh and often humid environment, pleasant for jogging.

Q: Is parking available, and do I need to make reservations?
A: Yes, parking is available, for instance, at the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4226174" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Parking in front of the Lillas River&lt;/a&gt;. Due to the park's popularity, especially during peak seasons like autumn, reservations for parking are often necessary. It's advisable to check the park's official website for current requirements before your visit.

Q: Are there any circular running routes in the park?
A: Yes, many routes in the park are circular, allowing you to start and end at the same point. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20143130" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Parking in front of the Lillas River – Tejera Negra Beech Forest loop from Aparcamiento Hayedo Tejera Negra&lt;/a&gt;, which follows the Lillas River through the dense beech forest.

Q: What do other runners enjoy most about jogging in National Park del Hayedo Tejera Negra?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 400 visitors. Runners often praise the stunning natural surroundings, the unique ancient beech forest, and the diverse terrain that offers both challenging ascents and peaceful riverside paths.

Q: Can I spot any wildlife while running in the park?
A: The park is home to diverse fauna. While jogging, you might encounter roe deer, foxes, wild cats, and wild boars. It's also a great place for birdwatching, with diurnal birds of prey like the golden eagle and red kite soaring overhead.

Q: Are there any longer, more challenging running routes for experienced runners?
A: Absolutely. For seasoned runners seeking a greater challenge, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31514860" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cerro Gordo – Mirador de Peñas Llanas loop from Ermita de Hontanares&lt;/a&gt; offer significant elevation gain over 8 miles (12.8 km), typically taking over two hours to complete. Another option is the Senda del Robledal, a longer circular route of approximately 17 kilometers, starting from the Interpretation Center.

Q: What kind of trees and plants will I see along the running trails?
A: Beyond the dominant beech trees, the park boasts a rich variety of flora. You'll encounter yew, holly, birch, oak, hazel, and pine, especially in shadier valleys. The undergrowth features heather, broom, and various thickets, adding to the natural beauty of your run.

Q: Is public transport available to reach the National Park del Hayedo Tejera Negra?
A: While the region is primarily accessed by car, some local bus services might connect to nearby towns. However, direct public transport to the park's trailheads is limited. It's recommended to check local transport schedules or consider carpooling for easier access to the running routes.
tips: Viewpoint in Castilla-La Mancha located near the natural reserve Parque Natural del Hayedo de Tejera Negra and the Alto de la Escaleruela peak.

In the Tejera Negra forests are the southernmost beech forests in Europe, included in the protected area of the Sierra Norte de Guadalajara Natural Park, together with other small stands of beech in Montejo de la Sierra, in Madrid, Riofrío de Riaza, in Segovia, and the Vallcaneres ravine, in Tarragona, known as the Fageda del Retaule, the Retablo Beech Forest. The Tejera Negra forest was declared a World Heritage Site by UNESCO in 2017.
